Show HN：我与 Claude 和 Codex 在 3 周内构建了一个社交媒体管理工具

Show HN: I built a social media management tool in 3 weeks with Claude and Codex

github.com 作者 JanSchu 分数 177 评论 120 2026/04/13 17:26
打开 HN 原帖 打开原始文章

中文摘要

  1. 面向创作者、机构和中小企业的开源社交媒体管理。
  2. BrightBean Studio 是一个开源、可自托管的社交媒体管理平台，专为创作者、代理商和中小型企业而构建。它的功能与 Sendible、SocialPilot 或 ContentStudio 的功能相同，但免费且没有每个席位、每个频道或每个工作空间的限制。从单个多工作区仪表板规划、撰写、安排、批准、发布和监控 Facebook、Instagram、LinkedIn、TikTok、YouTube、Pinterest、Threads、Bluesky、Google Business Profile 和 Mastodon 上的内容。它适合在同一屋檐下管理多个客户帐户的人们，他们宁愿拥有自己的社交堆栈，也不愿每月向 SaaS 供应商支付 100-300 美元。每个功能都可供每个用户使用。没有付费等级，没有功能门，没有追加销售。您可以在 Heroku、Render 或 Railway 上一键部署它，通过 Docker 在您自己的 VPS 上运行它，或者在本地运行它。所有平台集成都使用您自己的开发人员凭证直接与官方第一方 API 对话，因此没有聚合中间人，没有供应商锁定，也没有第三方介于您和您的数据之间。特征
  3. 多工作空间和团队无限的组织→工作空间→成员。具有自定义角色、邀请和外部协作者的单独客户端角色的精细 RBAC。
  4. 内容编辑器 丰富的编辑器，具有每个平台的标题/媒体覆盖、版本历史记录、可重复使用的模板、内容类别和标签、看板创意板。
  5. 日历和日程安排 可视化日历，每个帐户每周定期发帖时段，以及自动将帖子分配到下一个可用时段的命名队列。

Original Summary

  1. Open-source social media management for creators, agencies, and SMBs.
  2. BrightBean Studio is an open-source, self-hostable social media management platform built for creators, agencies and SMBs. It does what Sendible, SocialPilot, or ContentStudio do, but free and without per-seat, per-channel, or per-workspace limits. Plan, compose, schedule, approve, publish, and monitor content across Facebook, Instagram, LinkedIn, TikTok, YouTube, Pinterest, Threads, Bluesky, Google Business Profile, and Mastodon from a single multi-workspace dashboard. It's for people managing many client accounts under one roof who'd rather own their social stack than pay $100–300/month to a SaaS vendor. Every feature is available to every user. No paid tier, no feature gate, no upsell. You can deploy it with a one-click button on Heroku, Render, or Railway, run it on your own VPS via Docker, or run it locally. All platform integrations talk directly to the official first-party APIs using your own developer credentials, so there's no aggregator middleman, no vendor lock-in, and no third party sitting between you and your data. Features
  3. Multi-workspace & teams Unlimited orgs → workspaces → members. Granular RBAC with custom roles, invitations, and a separate Client role for external collaborators.
  4. Content composer Rich editor with per-platform caption/media overrides, version history, reusable templates, content categories & tags, a Kanban idea board.
  5. Calendar & scheduling Visual calendar with recurring weekly posting slots per account and named queues that auto-assign posts to the next available slot.

评论（默认中文）

FireInsight 2026/04/13 19:22
我确实处于此类工具的“目标市场”中，但在之前评估过该工具后，我发现其质量和自托管体验非常糟糕，而专有的免费增值产品仍然是一种更好的体验。

不过，由于整个“三周内编码的氛围”的事情，我什至犹豫是否要看看这个项目。听到这个消息对我来说，这并不严重，也没有经过战斗考验，可能会得不到维护等。您认为这些担忧合理吗？

I am genuinely in the "target market" for a tool such as this, but having evaluated one previously I found the quality and self-hosting experience to be pretty bad, and that a proprietary freemium product was still a better experience.

I'm hesitant to even take a look at this project due to the whole "vibe coded in 3 weeks" thing, though. Hearing that says to me that this is not serious or battle-tested and might go unmaintained or such. Do you think these are valid concerns to have?

spicyusername 2026/04/13 19:28
我们正在进入一个软件交付成本低廉的时代。基本上任何想法都可以由一两个人在一两个月内实现 MVP。业界很快就知道下一组瓶颈是什么，因为瓶颈不再是编写代码。

规划、设计、管理协调、寻找客户、与其他产品集成、等待审核等等。基本上所有无法自动化的人力工作都消失了。

您的评论提醒我将建立支持团队添加到列表中。

We're entering an era where the delivering of software is cheap. Basically any idea can have an MVP implemented by one or two people in just a month or two now. Very quickly the industry is learning what the next set of bottlenecks are, now that the bottleneck is no longer writing code.

Planning, design, management alignment, finding customers, integrating with other products, waiting for review, etc. Basically all the human stuff that can't be automated away.

Your comment reminds me to add building a support team to the list.

written-beyond 2026/04/13 19:38
我同意，软件（软件初创公司）一直是投资者的金童，因为它与硬件或任何其他物理产品相比非常便宜。

无论法学硕士是否参与，好的软件都是昂贵的，因为你需要有人承担责任。大公司会节省一笔钱，因为承担上述责任所需的人员可能会更少，但与整体计划相比，这可能只是边际节省。

I agree, software (software startups) has always been the golden child of investors because of how cheap it is compared to hardware or any other physical good.

Good software is expensive regardless of the involvement of LLMs because you need someone to take responsibility. Large companies will save a buck because there may be fewer people needed to take said responsibility, but it's probably a marginal saving compared to the overall scheme of things.

apsurd 2026/04/14 00:03
维护山上更多的代理代码。

争论的焦点是代理可以维护代理所构建的内容。但必须有人来管理系统复杂性的爆炸式增长。

我刚刚辞去了工作，因为运输代理代码自上而下的爆炸式增长。我认为这行不通，而且我也不希望我的工作是维护别人的 50 倍代码输出。

Maintenance of mountains more agentic code.

The argument is the agents can maintain what the agents build. But someone has to manage the explosion in system complexity.

I just quit my job because there was top down explosion of shipping agentic code. I don't think it's going to work and I don't want my job to be maintaining someone else's 50x code output.

我同意。这个项目并没有颠覆定价过高的产品/SaaS。

例如。 Buffer 对每个社交媒体帐户每年收取约 50 美元的费用，这为您提供了无限数量的协作用户帐户。他们的单用户计划甚至更便宜。

I agree. It's not like this project is disrupting an overpriced product/SaaS.

E.g. Buffer charges around $50 per year per social media account, which gives you an unlimited number of collaborating user accounts. And their single user plans are even cheaper.

I don't see how self-hosting would be a worthy investment of your time/effort in this case, unless you are in some grossly mismanaged organization where you have several devops engineers paid for doing literally nothing.

swasheck 2026/04/13 20:55
我认为我们需要编码（或完善）“氛围代码”的含义。我最初的印象是，它被用来描述一个有想法但缺乏开发/工程技能的人通过代理人利用法学硕士来创建机制以实现他们的想法的过程。现在看来，如果它有人工智能的暗示，那么它就是“振动编码”。

讽刺的是，我没有读这篇文章，因为我现在才来看评论，看看它是否被认定为 AI slop，所以我不知道这属于哪个领域

i think we need to encode (or refine) what we mean by “vibe code.” my original impression was that it was used to describe the process whereby someone with an idea but lacking development/engineering skills leveraged llm via an agent to create the mechanics to bring their idea to fruition. anymore it seems like if it has the hint of AI then it’s “vibe coded.”

ironically, i didn’t read the article because i come to comments now to see if its been identified as AI slop, so i don’t know which area this falls into

dghlsakjg 2026/04/13 21:43
确实。最初的氛围编码推文非常明确地提到“忘记代码的存在”和“一次性周末项目”（https://x.com/karpathy/status/1886192184808149383）

然而现在，许多人只是用它来表示任何人工智能辅助编码。

有人说“vibe 编码”是指用于抓取某些页面的一次性脚本。其他人用它来表示由 Claude 团队构建的经过代码审查的应用程序。

它是如此广泛以至于在这一点上毫无意义

Definitely. The original vibe coding tweet is pretty explicit about “forgetting the code exists”, and “throwaway weekend projects” (https://x.com/karpathy/status/1886192184808149383)

Now however, many people just use it to mean any ai-assisted coding.

One person says “vibe coding” to mean a throwaway script to scrape some page. Others use it to mean a code reviewed app built by a team using Claude.

It is so broad as to be meaningless at this point

在 HN 中查看
63stack 2026/04/13 19:42
在我看来，与他人分享你制作的一些小程序以从中受益的时代已经结束了。

您可以自己进行振动编码。如果您的要求更窄（例如，您只需要支持 3 个网络，而不是 12 个），那么您最终会得到开发时间更短（可能不到一天）的东西，它的问题面会更小，并且会更好地满足您的特定需求。如果您关注法学硕士正在做的事情，它也会更容易维护或进一步扩展。

安全漏洞的表面也变得更窄，因为你“只”必须信任LLM（这仍然是一个巨大的要求，但仍然比LLM + 1个随机人更好）。

The era of sharing some small programs that you made with others to benefit from is over imo.

You can just vibe code it yourself. If your requirements are narrower (eg. you only need support for 3 networks and not 12), you will end up with something that takes less time to develop (possibly less than a day), it will have a smaller surface for problems, and it will be much better tailored to your specific needs. If you pay attention to what the LLM is doing it will also be easier to maintain or extend further.

The surface for security vulnerabilities also gets narrower, since you "only" have to trust the LLM (which is still a huge ask, but still better than LLM + 1 random person).

在 HN 中查看
ninkendo 2026/04/13 20:50
> 在我看来，与他人分享自己制作的一些小程序以从中受益的时代已经结束了。 > 您可以自己进行振动编码。

+1。

我现在全职使用的密码管理器是“Kenpass”，它只有一个用户：我。我在 iOS/macOS/Linux 上都有它，浏览器扩展、CLI 和（本机，无电子）UI，通过我的wireguard 隧道与我的家庭实验室服务器同步，它涵盖了我的所有用例。我花了大约一周的时间（总共几个小时，分散开来。）我觉得没有理由与任何人分享它，它完全满足了我的需要，我只需要为自己修复我发现的错误。

我们确实生活在一个疯狂的时代。

> 如果您关注法学硕士正在做的事情，它也会更容易进一步维护或扩展

这是另一个不错的部分：我实际上非常喜欢风水重构代码来满足我的口味，并且我给了法学硕士的代码一堆重构过程，本质上“只是为了好玩”。我对代码库有足够的了解，有时我会自己实现功能，而不是让法学硕士来做，如果我有心情的话。但我可能永远没有时间或精力从头开始这样一个项目……一次性完成最初的 MVP 是一个巨大的推动。

> The era of sharing some small programs that you made with others to benefit from is over imo. > You can just vibe code it yourself.

+1.

The password manager I use full time now is “Kenpass”, which has exactly one user: me. I have it on iOS/macOS/Linux, browser extensions, CLI and (native, no electron) UI for each, syncs with my homelab server over my wireguard tunnel, and it covers all my use cases. Took me maybe a week (a few hours total, spread around.) I feel no reason to share it with anyone, it does exactly what I need and I only need to fix the bugs I find, for myself.

We’re really living in crazy times.

> If you pay attention to what the LLM is doing it will also be easier to maintain or extend further

That's another nice part: I actually really enjoy feng-shui refactoring code to fit my tastes, and I've given the LLM's code a bunch of refactoring passes essentially "just for fun". I understand the codebase enough that sometimes I implement features myself instead of having the LLM do it, if I'm in the mood to. But I'd probably never have the time or energy to start such a project from scratch... having the initial MVP done in essentially one shot was a huge boost.

ninkendo 2026/04/14 00:26
是的，它确实需要一个每年 100 美元的开发者帐户。但就是这样：一旦你拥有了它，该应用程序就没有时间限制或任何限制。我可以通过 Xcode 将其安装到我的设备（无线），然后就完成了。

如果您想自己制作其他应用程序，那么每年 100 美元可能只值得。我对适合我的家人的应用程序有其他想法，所以我认为这是值得的。但我承认，我必须付钱，这很糟糕。

It does require a developer account at $100 a year, yes. But that's it: Once you have that, the app is not time-limited or anything. I can just install it to my device (wirelessly) via Xcode and I'm done.

It's probably only worth the $100/year if there are other apps you want to make for yourself though. I have other ideas for apps for my family, so I figured it's worth the cost. It sucks that I have to pay at all though, I admit.

deaux 2026/04/14 00:42
这很有趣——我认为那些没有通过App Store但处于“开发者模式”的应用程序是有时间限制的，但也许我把它与“与家人分享”而不是纯粹的个人独奏混淆了 使用。

听起来你是用 Swift 写的（？），它在自动填充代码方面也做得很好吗？这就是我预计它可能会遇到的困难，因为它可能是一个罕见且特别原生的功能。但是 API 应该针对 iOS 进行良好的标准化。

非常酷和鼓舞人心的东西！

That's interesting - I thought apps that didn't go through the App Store but were in "developer mode" were time-limited, but maybe I confused it with "share with family" rather than pure personal solo use.

Sounds like you wrote it in Swift(?), did it also do a good job at the autofill code? That's what I'd expect it to potentially struggle at, as it could be a rare and particularly native feature. But then the APIs should be well standardized for iOS.

Very cool and inspiring stuff!

在 HN 中查看
ninkendo 2026/04/14 00:52
自动填充代码实际上是 Safari 的，而不是密码管理器的。密码管理器基本上只需要实现 ASCredentialProviderViewController （https://developer.apple.com/documentation/authenticationserv...）并包含“自动填充凭据提供商”权利，Safari 会询问您是否要使用 Kenpass 登录网站和应用程序。

对于火狐（桌面）来说有点困难，你必须实际实现登录框检测。它的工作时间大约有一半，但我不太介意，因为仅使用剪贴板没什么大不了的。即使在使用 1Password 时，我也经常发现密码自动填充功能如此糟糕（或者不断地要求我保存我不想保存的密码，或者甚至不是登录框的东西），以至于我实际上非常讨厌它。创建登录名时，我更喜欢采取明确的步骤来激活我的密码管理器来执行此操作，并且当提示登录时，当简单的检测不起作用时，简单地复制/粘贴并不是什么大问题。

The autofill code is actually Safari's, not the password manager. The password manager basically just needs to impmlement ASCredentialProviderViewController (https://developer.apple.com/documentation/authenticationserv...) and include the "AutoFill Credential Provider" entitlement and safari will ask if you want to sign in with Kenpass for websites and apps.

For firefox (desktop) it's a bit harder, you have to actually implement login box detection. It works like half the time, but I don't really mind much, because it's not a huge deal to just use the clipboard. Even when using 1Password I would so often find that the password autofill was so broken (or would constantly offer me to save passwords I don't want to, or for things that aren't even login boxes) that I actually actively hated it. When creating logins, I much prefer taking an explicit step to activate my password manager to do so, and when prompting for logging in, it's not a huge deal to simply copy/paste when the simple detection doesn't work.

ElFitz 2026/04/13 20:59
> 安全漏洞的范围也变得更窄，因为你“只”必须信任 LLM（这仍然是一个巨大的要求，但仍然比 LLM + 1 个随机人更好）。

最重要的是，任何此类漏洞大多都是低价值的：n 个不同的实现，每个都有自己的特性，其中 90% 服务于一个人。

> The surface for security vulnerabilities also gets narrower, since you "only" have to trust the LLM (which is still a huge ask, but still better than LLM + 1 random person).

On top of which, any such vulnerabilities will be mostly low value: n different implementations, each with their own idiosyncrasies, 90% of them serving one person.

bdangubic 2026/04/13 21:08
我觉得我们这个行业 真的 需要快速掌握术语/名称。对我来说，“vibe coded”意味着“100%（或接近）这是由 LLM 编写的，我对技术或黑客攻击或与此领域相关的任何内容没有丝毫线索。”如果这里（或其他任何地方）都是这种情况，我不会用 10 英尺长的杆子碰它（即使作者很诚实）。像“法学硕士协助”之类的东西将是另一回事。
I think our industry really needs to get on top of terms / names and fast. To me, “vibe coded” means “100% (or close to it) of this was written by LLM and I do not have a slightest clue about technology or hacking or anything related to this domain.” if this is the case here (or anywhere else) I am not touching it with a 10-foot pole (even with honesty of the author). something like “LLM assisted” would be a whole other thing.
在 HN 中查看
TrackerFF 2026/04/13 22:23
对我来说，“vibe 编码”就像 Karpathy 在那条推文中写道：你完全屈服于模型，接受一切，从不查看代码中的更改。仅提供提示并检查生成的产品。

但是，当然，这并不是大多数程序员使用的方式——至少我想是这样。一件事是，当零编程知识的人以这种方式编写代码时，但对于严肃的（阅读：大）产品，我想你仍然被迫做一些簿记。

To me "vibe coding" is like Karpathy wrote in that tweet: You completely surrender to the model, accept everything, never look at the changes in code. Only feed prompts and check the resulting product.

But, of course, that's not how most programmers use - at least I'd like to think. One thing is when people with zero programming knowledge vibe code something in that fashion, but for serious (read: big) products I'd like to think that you're still forced to do some book keeping.

在 HN 中查看
jrm4 2026/04/13 23:26
我的直觉是——当然这些担忧是有道理的，但是，特别是对于像这样的“新奇”软件项目，我真的很惊讶地看到“人类”和“振动编码”之间的重大质量差异。

我认为“新奇”的意思是；这不是低级 C 内存管理的位翻转；无论好坏，这种东西已经建立在网络已经存在的一层又一层的基础之上。

My gut is -- of course these concerns are valid, but, especially with "newfangled" software projects like this, I'd genuinely be surprised to see major quality differences between "human" and "vibe-coded."

I think what I mean by "newfangled" is; this isn't a low-level C memory managed bit flipping thing; this is the sort of thing that's already built on top of layer of layer of the cruft that the web already is, for better or worse.

slake 2026/04/14 11:30
我其实很喜欢2006年的推特。由于它的默默无闻，几乎令人愉快。

- 仅 140 个字符 - 增加添加到 slop 中的字符。简洁是灵魂 - 没有“为你”算法。您必须找到并关注您想关注的人，然后阅读他们的所有帖子 - RTing 顾名思义，就是在对方的推文前面添加 RT。 - 有一个“喜欢”图标（我想 - 不记得了） - 没有付费“已验证”帐户。你必须通过不同的方式向你的官方推特账户发出信号。 - 随机点击诱饵内容没有货币化。 - Twitter 上的广告并没有那么具有侵入性。作为一种货币化策略，这种策略仍然有效。 - 你只发现了人，却没有发现内容。

I actually like 2006 twitter. Almost pleasant because of its obscurity.

- Only 140 characters - Increasing that added to the slop. Brevity was soul - No 'For you' algo. You had to find and follow the people you wanted to follow and then read all of their posts - RTing it was literally adding RT in front of the other person's tweet. - There was a 'like' icon (I think - don't remember) - There was no paid 'verified' accounts. You had to signal your official twitter account through different means. - There was no monetization for random clickbait ragebait content. - Ads are not that intrusive on twitter. That as a monetization strategy still works. - You only discovered people and not content.

在 HN 中查看
JanSchu 2026/04/13 17:28
我想测试人工智能编码工具可以在生产项目中发挥多大作用。不是原型。一个社交媒体管理平台，具有 12 个第一方 API 集成、多租户身份验证、加密凭证存储、后台作业处理、审批工作流程和统一收件箱。这个范围通常会让独立开发者忙上一年的大部分时间。我在三周内发货了。

在编写任何代码之前，我花时间研究详细的规范、架构文档和风格指南。所有公众： https://github.com/brightbeanxyz/brightbean-studio/tree/main...

我将规范分解为可以在多个代理之间并行运行的任务，以及具有必须首先合并的依赖项的任务。这个计划步骤就是整个游戏。没有它，特工就会造成混乱。

我使用 Opus 4.6（Claude Code）来规划和构建后端和 UI 的第一遍。 Opus 更好地掌握大上下文，并更可靠地做出跨文件的架构决策。然后我使用 Codex 5.3 来挑战每个实现、暴露安全问题并捕获错误。两者之间的代币支出大致持平。

AI 编码效果良好的地方：Django 模型、视图、序列化器、标准 CRUD。为 Facebook 和 LinkedIn 等记录完善的 API 提供模块。 Tailwind 布局和 HTMX 交互。测试一代。跨文件重构，当我重构权限系统时，Opus 特别擅长跨模型、视图和模板进行级联更改。

失败的地方：TikTok 的内容发布 API 的文档很差，而且有不寻常的两步上传流程。这两种工具都一次又一次地自信地生成错误的代码。多租户权限逻辑生成的代码适用于单个工作区，但会在多工作区设置中跨租户泄露数据。这些错误通过了测试，这使得它们变得危险。 OAuth 边缘情况（例如令牌刷新、撤销权限和特定于平台的错误代码）都需要手动工作。快乐路径很好，但防御代码则不然。后台任务编排（重试逻辑、速率限制退避、错误处理）也需要手工编写。

我低估了一件事：如果没有专门的 UI 设计，获得一致的用户体验是残酷的。所有功能都在那里，但屏幕不直观，并且某些流程根本无法通过 UI 访问。 80% 的功能在 20% 的时间内有效。剩下的 80% 则用于完善并让体验真正可用。

该项目是 AGPL-3.0 下的开源项目。 12 个平台集成，全部为第一方 API。 Django 5.x + HTMX + Alpine.js + Tailwind CSS 4 + PostgreSQL。没有 Redis。 Docker Compose 部署，4 个容器。

向我询问有关规范驱动方法、平台 API 怪癖或我如何在两个模型之间分配工作的任何信息。

I wanted to test how far AI coding tools could take a production project. Not a prototype. A social media management platform with 12 first-party API integrations, multi-tenant auth, encrypted credential storage, background job processing, approval workflows, and a unified inbox. The scope would normally keep a solo developer busy for the better part of a year. I shipped it in 3 weeks.

Before writing any code, I spent time on detailed specs, an architecture doc, and a style guide. All public: https://github.com/brightbeanxyz/brightbean-studio/tree/main...

I broke the specs into tasks that could run in parallel across multiple agents versus tasks with dependencies that had to merge first. This planning step was the whole game. Without it, the agents produce a mess.

I used Opus 4.6 (Claude Code) for planning and building the first pass of backend and UI. Opus holds large context better and makes architectural decisions across files more reliably. Then I used Codex 5.3 to challenge every implementation, surface security issues, and catch bugs. Token spend was roughly even between the two.

Where AI coding worked well: Django models, views, serializers, standard CRUD. Provider modules for well-documented APIs like Facebook and LinkedIn. Tailwind layouts and HTMX interactions. Test generation. Cross-file refactoring, where Opus was particularly good at cascading changes across models, views, and templates when I restructured the permission system.

Where it fell apart: TikTok's Content Posting API has poor docs and an unusual two-step upload flow. Both tools generated wrong code confidently, over and over. Multi-tenant permission logic produced code that worked for a single workspace but leaked data across tenants in multi-workspace setups. These bugs passed tests, which is what made them dangerous. OAuth edge cases like token refresh, revoked permissions, and platform-specific error codes all needed manual work. Happy path was fine, defensive code was not. Background task orchestration (retry logic, rate-limit backoff, error handling) also required writing by hand.

One thing I underestimated: Without dedicated UI designs, getting a consistent UX was brutal. All the functionality was there, but screens were unintuitive and some flows weren't reachable through the UI at all. 80% of features worked in 20% of the time. The remaining 80% went to polish and making the experience actually usable.

The project is open source under AGPL-3.0. 12 platform integrations, all first-party APIs. Django 5.x + HTMX + Alpine.js + Tailwind CSS 4 + PostgreSQL. No Redis. Docker Compose deploy, 4 containers.

Ask me anything about the spec-driven approach, platform API quirks, or how I split work between the two models.

dontwannahearit 2026/04/13 19:40
有多少规范本身来自法学硕士？开发进度表 https://github.com/brightbeanxyz/brightbean-studio/blob/main... 例如，有非常人工智能外观的估计，我可以在architecture.md文件中看到一个提交，该提交专门将em-dashes更改为普通破折号（https://github.com/brightbeanxyz/brightbean-studio/commit/74...）这表明你想让它看起来不那么由 LLM 生成？

我不是要谴责，而是要了解你们制定需求的过程是什么。显然这是在法学硕士的帮助下完成的，但改进过程是怎样的呢？

How much of the specs themselves came from the LLM? The development schedule https://github.com/brightbeanxyz/brightbean-studio/blob/main... has very AI-looking estimates for exampl and I can see a commit in the architecture.md file which is exclusively changing em-dashes to normal dashes (https://github.com/brightbeanxyz/brightbean-studio/commit/74...) which suggests you wanted to make it seem less LLM-generated?

I ask, not to condemn, but to find out what your process was for developing the requirements. Clearly it was done with LLM help but what was the refinement process?

在 HN 中查看
JanSchu 2026/04/13 19:54
规范文档也是由 Claude 编写（经过多次迭代）并进行了大量手动添加。我花了整整 4 天的时间才将规格达到我满意的水平。

我做的一件主要事情是利用 Claude 的深入研究功能来很好地了解其他工具提供的功能（功能、集成等）。

然后，规范文档中的每个功能都通过手动建议和我拍摄的其他工具的屏幕截图进行了完善。

The spec document was also written by Claude (over many iteration) and lots of manual additions. It took me tho 4 full days to get the specs to the level I was happy with.

One main thing I did was to use the deep research feature of Claude to get a good understanding of what other tools are offering (features, integrations etc.)

Then each feature in the specs document got refined with manual suggestions and screenshots of other tools that I took.

drabbiticus 2026/04/13 21:25
感谢分享！

> 在编写任何代码之前，我花时间研究详细的规范、架构文档和风格指南。所有公众： https://github.com/brightbeanxyz/brightbean-studio/tree/main...

> 我花了整整 4 天的时间才将规格达到我满意的水平。

当我单击历史记录时，我只看到这些文档的一次提交。您是否愿意分享您与法学硕士（在要点或存储库中）进行的部分或全部对话，这些对话导致了这些架构文档？如果你不能理解，但我相信这对于那些试图理解做这样的事情的过程以及有助于有效地推进过程的导轨类型的人们来说将是非常有指导意义的。

Thanks for sharing!

> Before writing any code, I spent time on detailed specs, an architecture doc, and a style guide. All public: https://github.com/brightbeanxyz/brightbean-studio/tree/main...

> It took me tho 4 full days to get the specs to the level I was happy with.

When I click on history there I see only a single commit for these docs. Would you be willing to share some or all of the conversation you had with the LLM (in a gist or in the repo) that led to these architecture docs? Understand if you can't, but I'm sure it would be super instructive for people trying to understand the process of doing something like this and the types of guide rails that help to move the process forward productively.

vladsanchez 2026/04/13 22:08
首先，祝贺您取得的成就并发挥您的 AI+Python+WebDev 才能。

这难道不是 SaaS 末日的证明吗？是什么阻止人们对 BrightBean 做同样的事情？是什么阻止了拥有一点领域知识和 200 多美元 Claude 的人克隆您的应用程序并构建另一个填补空白、略有改进的内容联合版本并进入市场？当任何人都可以延续这个循环时，是否值得将其推向市场？

我真的很想知道你的想法。

First, congrats on your accomplishment(s) and leveraging your AI+Python+WebDev talents.

Isn't this a SaaS-pocaplyse testament? What's stopping anyone from doing the same to BrightBean? What's stopping anyone with a little of domain knowledge and a $200+ Claude to clone your app and build yet another gap-filling, slightly improved content-syndication version and go-to-market? Is it worth taking it to the market when anyone can perpetuate the cycle?

I'm genuinely interested in knowing your thoughts.

在 HN 中查看
JanSchu 2026/04/14 04:01
我个人的看法是，未来将普通软件货币化将极其困难。要么您的发行需要非常强大的优势和独特的角度，要么您必须构建一个无法由可以构建软件的代理复制的产品。

这将是两种类型的产品。

1. 所需技术不在编码代理底层模型的训练数据分布范围内的产品。这通常是非常前沿的。

2. 使用数据/见解为编码代理无法访问的客户创造价值的产品。

这是我能想到的唯一抽象的护城河，其余的将是一场逐底竞赛

My personal opinion is that it will be extremely difficult in the future to monetize plain software. Either you need a very strong edge and unique angle for your distribution or you have to build a product that cannot be reproduced by agents that can build software.

This will be 2 types of products.

1. A product which requires tech that is not inside the training data distribution of the models underlying coding agents. This is usually then very cutting edge.

2. A product that uses data/insights to generate value for a customer to which a coding agent has no access.

These are the only abstract moats I can think of, the rest will be a race to the bottom

在 HN 中查看
SeriousM 2026/04/14 13:20
第三个也是非常有效的一个是： 你处于一个很好的市场，有很好的联系并融入客户流程，你的客户更关心你作为一个人/顾问/交付，而不是经历更换你的麻烦。
A third and very valid one is: You are in a nice he market, be very well connected and buried into customers process and your customer care more about you as a person/consultant/delivery than going though the hassle of replacing you.

DrammBA 2026/04/13 22:37
> 这难道不是 SaaS-pocaplyse 的遗嘱吗？是什么阻止人们对 BrightBean 做同样的事情？

它是开源的也没有帮助，所以很容易恶意/破坏它。

在 HN 中查看
qingcharles 2026/04/14 00:15
我为客户构建了一个非常非常相似的东西，可以按计划将他们的内容发布到大约 9 个不同的社交网络上。这是我的第一个主要的振动编码应用程序——我通常对函数或小型应用程序进行振动编码。与 Claude [0] 一起花了大约两个小时，仅在层中构建功能，并在进行过程中测试每一层。如果我按照 2022 年的风格，坚持不懈地写下去，我可能需要一个月的时间才能写完。

它已经完美运行了几个月，没有出现任何错误。事实上，怪异的好。尽管如此，我仍然感到紧张，每天早上都会检查一下。

它主要是使用 SQLite 数据库在 .NET 10 Razor 中构建为 Web 应用程序。

社交网络的 API 还比较模糊。正如OP提到的，有些记录很糟糕。有些很难访问。我使用驱动浏览器向 Twitter 发帖，但他们最近打开了 API，这很好。

[0] 我在 Github Copilot 中使用了 Claude，因此总成本低于每月 10 美元的积分。

I built something very, very similar for a client to post their content on schedule to about 9 different social networks. It was my first major vibe-coded app -- I normally vibe-code a function or small apps. Took about two hours with Claude [0] by just building up the functionality in layers, testing each layer as we went. If I'd rawdogged it, 2022-style, it would probably have taken me a month to write.

It's been running flawlessly for months without a single error. In fact, spooky good. I still feel nervous about it, though and check it every morning.

It is built mostly as a web app in .NET 10 Razor with SQLite db.

The APIs for the social networks are the hazy bit. As OP mentions, some are badly documented. Some are a pain to get access to. I was using a driven browser to post to Twitter, but they opened their API recently, which was nice.

[0] I used Claude in Github Copilot, so total cost was less than the $10/month in credits.

在 HN 中查看
SeriousM 2026/04/14 13:30
最近，我构建了一个“azure devops 部署中心”工具，只是因为微软无法使其 ui 大规模可用。

该工具一目了然地在一处显示所有准备就绪的管道、构建、构建代理和批准。对于大多数操作，我使用公共 api，但审批时我使用内部 api，因为自 4 年来它们仍然不公开。

该工具运行如此流畅，这也让我感到害怕！我的猜测是 API 非常稳定，可以在未来 10 年内使用。

Recently I built a "azure devops deployment central" tool just because microsoft is unable to make their ui usable at scale.

The tool shows all pipelines, builds, buildagents and approval ready in one place at a glance. For the most actions I use the public api but approval I use the internal api because they are still not public, since 4 years.

The tool runs so smooth it scares me too! My guess is that the apis are so stable that it will work for the next 10 years.

在 HN 中查看
mrsekut 2026/04/13 19:26
那是一篇有趣的文章。我有几个关于工作流程的问题。

1. 您提到并行开发任务——您实际上同时运行了多少个代理？您是否曾经遇到这样的情况：即使增加了并行度，合并和审查也成为瓶颈，并且进一步增加数量并不能加快速度？

2. 我真的很认同“80% 的功能在 20% 的时间里完成，然后 80% 的时间进行润色”的想法。您是否也在最后的打磨阶段使用了人工智能？换句话说，你有没有展示AI屏幕截图并解释一下？另外，当你回头看时，你是否觉得如果你把最初的规范写得更仔细一些，你就能更快地完成工作？

That was an interesting article. I have a few questions about the workflow.

1. You mentioned developing tasks in parallel—how many agents were you actually running at the same time? Did you ever reach a point where, even if you increased the degree of parallelism, merging and reviews became the bottleneck, and increasing the number further didn’t speed things up?

2. I really relate to the idea of “80% of features in 20% of the time, then 80% on polish.” Did you use AI for this final polishing phase as well? In other words, did you show the AI screenshots of the screens and explain them? Also, when looking back, do you feel that if you had written the initial specifications more carefully, you could have completed the work faster?

在 HN 中查看
JanSchu 2026/04/13 20:15
我所做的是将开发分成不同的层，这些层必须一个接一个地完成，因为功能是相互构建的。每层都有独立的并行运行的工作流。每个工作流都是 Claude 代码中的一个独立的工作树/会话

首先，我触发了每层的所有工作流，并使它们达到我满意的完成水平。然后，当您移动到下一个工作流时，您将一个又一个地合并（在 github 中使用 @codex 挑战实现和变基）。

大致是这样的：

第 0 层 - 项目脚手架

第 1 层 — 核心功能 流 A — 内容管道 B 组别——社交平台提供商 Stream C — 媒体库 Stream D——通知系统 Stream E — 设置 UI

                        T-0.1 (Scaffolding)
                              │
                        T-0.2 (Core Models + Auth)
                              │
          ┌───────────────────┼───────────────────┬──────────────┐
          │                   │                   │              │
     Stream A            Stream B            Stream C       Stream D
     (Content)           (Providers)         (Media)        (Notifs)
          │                   │                   │              │
     T-1A.1 Composer    T-1B.1 FB/IG/LI    T-1C.1 Library  T-1D.1 Engine
          │              T-1B.2 Others           │              │
     T-1A.2 Calendar         │                   │         Stream E
          │                  │                   │         T-1E.1 Settings UI
     T-1A.3 Publisher ◄──────┘                   │
          │                                      │
          └──────────◄───────────────────────────┘
          (Publisher needs providers + media processing)
第 2 层 — 协作与参与 流 F — 批准和客户端门户 流 G — 收件箱 Stream H — 日历和作曲家增强功能 流 I — 客户引导

          Layer 1 complete
                │
    ┌───────────┼───────────┬──────────────┐
    │           │           │              │
 Stream F   Stream G    Stream H       Stream I
 (Approval  (Inbox)     (Calendar+     (Onboarding)
  + Portal)              Composer
    │                    enhance)
 T-2F.1 Approval
    │
 T-2F.2 Portal
因此，我确实并行运行了多达 4 个代理，但说实话，这是我的大脑能够处理的最大并行度，我真的觉得这里是瓶颈。

此外，您的令牌使用率非常高，因为您有这么多代理同时工作，因此我经常达到克劳德会话令牌限制，不得不等待下一个会话开始（我确实有 5x Max 计划）

What I did was to break the development into different layers which had to be completed after another, since the functionalities build on each other. Each layer had independent work streams which run in parallel. Each work stream was one independent worktree/session in Claude code

First I triggered all work streams per layer and brought them to a level of completion I was happy with. Then you merge one after another (challenge in github with the @codex the implementation and rebases when you move to the next work stream.

This is roughly how it looked like:

Layer 0 - Project Scaffolding

Layer 1 — Core Features Stream A — Content Pipeline Stream B — Social Platform Providers Stream C — Media Library Stream D — Notification System Stream E — Settings UI

                        T-0.1 (Scaffolding)
                              │
                        T-0.2 (Core Models + Auth)
                              │
          ┌───────────────────┼───────────────────┬──────────────┐
          │                   │                   │              │
     Stream A            Stream B            Stream C       Stream D
     (Content)           (Providers)         (Media)        (Notifs)
          │                   │                   │              │
     T-1A.1 Composer    T-1B.1 FB/IG/LI    T-1C.1 Library  T-1D.1 Engine
          │              T-1B.2 Others           │              │
     T-1A.2 Calendar         │                   │         Stream E
          │                  │                   │         T-1E.1 Settings UI
     T-1A.3 Publisher ◄──────┘                   │
          │                                      │
          └──────────◄───────────────────────────┘
          (Publisher needs providers + media processing)
Layer 2 — Collaboration & Engagement Stream F — Approval & Client Portal Stream G — Inbox Stream H — Calendar & Composer Enhancements Stream I — Client Onboarding

          Layer 1 complete
                │
    ┌───────────┼───────────┬──────────────┐
    │           │           │              │
 Stream F   Stream G    Stream H       Stream I
 (Approval  (Inbox)     (Calendar+     (Onboarding)
  + Portal)              Composer
    │                    enhance)
 T-2F.1 Approval
    │
 T-2F.2 Portal
Thus I did run up to 4 agents in parallel, but o be honest this is the max level of parallelism my brain was able to handle, I really felt like the bottleneck here.

Additionally, your token usage is very high since you are having so many agent do work at the same time, hence I very often reached my claude session token limits and had to wait for the next session to begin (I do have the 5x Max plan)

hyperionultra 2026/04/13 19:06
为什么使用 postgre 而不是经典的 mysql？
Why postgre instead of classic mysql?
在 HN 中查看
purerandomness 2026/04/13 19:12
MySQL 不允许您使用事务性 DDL 语句（更改、创建、索引等）。

如果您正在构建任何严肃的东西并且数据完整性很重要，请使用 Postgres。

Postgres 更加严格，而且一直如此。 MySQL 尝试引入几种严格模式来缓解它们所遇到的问题，但我始终建议使用 Postgres。

MySQL does not let you have transactional DDL statements (alter, create, index etc).

If you're building anything serious and your data integrity is important, use Postgres.

Postgres is much stricter, and always was. MySQL tried to introduce several strict modes to mitigate the problems that they had, but I would always recommend to use Postgres.

JanSchu 2026/04/13 19:20
我最初确实有数据科学背景，因此 python 通常是我的首选语言，并且已经有很多 django 的经验。这在审查人工智能代码以及必须判断架构等时很有帮助。

对于 hmtx，我只是想拥有一些轻量级的东西，不是很有侵入性，以保持事情简单和依赖性低。

在我看来，这是一个很好的考虑，可以降低我的人工智能代理的复杂性:-)

I do have originally a data science background, thus python is usually my go to language, and have a lot of experience with django already. This helps a lot when reviewing AI code and if you have to judge architecture, etc.

And for hmtx I simply wanted to have something lightweight that is not very invasive to keep things simple and dependencies low.

In my head this was a good consideration to keep complexity low for my AI agents :-)

canarias_mate 2026/04/14 10:01
通过测试的多租户错误是使用 AI codegen 快速交付的最可怕的部分。我们一直在致力于 Autonoma（https://www.getautonoma.com/）正是为此，人工智能代理通过像真实用户一样导航您的应用程序并捕获单元测试遗漏的内容来进行端到端测试。
Multi-tenant bugs slipping through tests is the scariest part of shipping fast with AI codegen. We've been working on Autonoma (https://www.getautonoma.com/) for exactly this, AI agents that do E2E testing by navigating your app like a real user and catching the stuff unit tests miss.
